sourceLecture: Reconstructive Ladder Dr Suzan Fazil Jaber
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man undergoes wide local excision of a soft-tissue sarcoma on the anterior thigh. The resulting defect measures 4 × 3 cm, the wound base is well vascularised granulation tissue, there is no exposed bone or tendon, wound edges can be advanced without tension, and the patient has no significant comorbidities. Swabs show no infection. Which reconstructive option best follows the principle of the reconstructive ladder?
options: Primary direct closure
Split-thickness skin graft
Random-pattern local flap
Free flap microsurgical reconstruction
Vacuum-assisted closure device as definitive management
explanation: The reconstructive ladder directs the surgeon to use the simplest technique that reliably achieves wound closure. A 4 × 3 cm defect with a healthy vascularised base and mobile wound edges that can be approximated without tension is best closed by primary direct closure — the lowest rung of the ladder. Higher-rung options (graft, flap, free flap) add morbidity and complexity unnecessarily when simple closure is feasible.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: primary direct closure is the simplest effective option when wound edges approximate without tension — the foundational principle of the reconstructive ladder.
A split-thickness skin graft is appropriate when primary closure is impossible but there is a vascularised recipient bed; it is not needed when direct closure is achievable.
A local flap involves donor-site morbidity and is reserved for defects that cannot be closed primarily or require structural tissue transfer.
Free flap microsurgical reconstruction is the highest rung of the ladder, reserved for complex defects with exposed vital structures or when simpler options have failed.
VAC (negative-pressure wound therapy) is an adjunct to promote healing or prepare a wound bed, not a definitive reconstruction, and should not replace primary closure when it is feasible.
clinicalPearl: The reconstructive ladder: always choose the simplest reliable method — primary closure before graft, graft before local flap, local flap before free flap.
keyClue: Well-vascularised wound base with mobile edges that can be approximated without tension
commonExamTrap: Jumping to a skin graft or flap when primary closure is entirely feasible; the ladder mandates starting at the bottom rung.
missedConcept: The reconstructive ladder principle: ascending complexity only when the simpler rung is inadequate — not selecting the most impressive technique.

sourceLecture: Reconstructive Ladder Dr Suzan Fazil Jaber
clinicalStem: A 60-year-old diabetic woman had a split-thickness skin graft applied to a 6 × 5 cm lower-leg venous ulcer five days ago. On review the graft appears pale, adherent in only two small areas, and the majority has become necrotic. Swabs taken at the time of grafting grew heavy Pseudomonas aeruginosa. She had no fever and her blood glucose was well controlled peri-operatively. Which factor most directly caused graft failure in this patient?
options: Heavy bacterial colonisation of the recipient bed preventing plasmatic imbibition and inosculation
Inadequate immobilisation allowing shear forces to disrupt neovascularisation
Haematoma formation beneath the graft blocking nutrient diffusion
Diabetes-related microvascular disease impairing graft revascularisation
Graft harvested too thick, preventing adequate diffusion in the early phase
explanation: Heavy bacterial colonisation — especially Pseudomonas aeruginosa at counts exceeding 10^5 organisms per gram — is a well-established cause of graft failure. Bacteria release proteolytic enzymes that digest fibrin and destroy the nutrient film essential for early plasmatic imbibition, and they physically disrupt the neovascularisation required for inosculation. The temporal correlation (heavy growth at grafting, most of graft necrotic by day 5) directly implicates infection as the primary cause here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Pseudomonas colonisation destroys the fibrin layer needed for plasmatic imbibition and prevents inosculation, the two stages of early graft take.
Shear forces are a significant cause of graft failure but there is no description of inadequate dressing or movement; the identified Pseudomonas heavy growth is the more direct finding.
Haematoma is a classic cause but there is no clinical description suggesting blood beneath the graft; the operative detail is absent and the swab result is the given clue.
Diabetes impairs healing but the question states blood glucose was well controlled and the swab result provides a more specific, direct explanation.
Graft thickness affects diffusion distance, but split-thickness grafts are thin by definition and there is no suggestion the harvest was incorrectly performed.
clinicalPearl: The three enemies of skin graft take: haematoma, seroma, and infection — Pseudomonas and beta-haemolytic Streptococcus are the most destructive pathogens.
keyClue: Heavy Pseudomonas aeruginosa on pre-operative swab with subsequent full-thickness graft necrosis by day 5
commonExamTrap: Attributing graft failure solely to diabetes when a specific infective organism is identified; the bacteriological finding is the direct mechanism.
missedConcept: Bacterial load >10^5 organisms/g prevents fibrin adhesion and neovascularisation, making graft take impossible regardless of other favourable conditions.

sourceLecture: Reconstructive Ladder Dr Suzan Fazil Jaber
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man underwent mandibular reconstruction with a free fibula osteocutaneous flap 8 hours ago. The nurse calls you urgently: the skin paddle is now dusky, cool, and has become turgid with a purple hue; capillary refill is 3 seconds centrally and the flap is tense on palpation. There is a Doppler signal present in the skin paddle. His general observations are stable. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Return urgently to theatre for flap exploration and venous anastomosis revision
Apply warm compresses and observe for 1 hour before re-assessing
Start intravenous heparin infusion and reassess in 30 minutes
Remove the sutures to relieve tension on the pedicle and monitor
Perform bedside leech application as definitive management
explanation: The clinical picture — duskiness, purple discolouration, turgidity, prolonged capillary refill with a present Doppler signal — is classic for venous congestion/thrombosis in a free flap. Venous insufficiency is the most common cause of free flap failure, and the critical window for salvage is within 2 hours of identifying the problem. The only definitive intervention is urgent surgical exploration and revision of the venous anastomosis; delay beyond this window leads to irreversible flap loss.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: urgent return to theatre is mandatory — venous thrombosis requires anastomosis revision within the salvage window (ideally <2 hours) to save the flap.
Warm compresses and observation waste the critical time window; venous congestion will progress to irreversible ischaemia if not surgically corrected urgently.
IV heparin alone cannot recanalize a mechanically thrombosed anastomosis and delays definitive surgical intervention; it has no role as sole treatment here.
Removing skin sutures may slightly relieve external compression but does not address venous anastomotic thrombosis, which is an internal vascular problem.

Leeches are an adjunct for venous congestion when no surgical option exists, but a free flap with acute congestion at 8 hours must be surgically explored first; leeches as definitive management risk flap loss.
clinicalPearl: Purple, turgid, cool free flap with present Doppler = venous congestion — return to theatre immediately; the salvage window is approximately 2 hours.
keyClue: Purple discolouration, turgidity, 3-second capillary refill at 8 hours post-operatively — classic venous congestion pattern
commonExamTrap: Confusing venous congestion (purple, turgid, present Doppler) with arterial insufficiency (pale, flat, absent Doppler); both require urgent re-exploration but the colour and Doppler distinguish them.
missedConcept: Post-operative free flap monitoring is a clinical emergency — venous thrombosis is the most common cause of flap failure and has a narrow salvage window.
redFlagReason: Acute venous congestion of a free flap is a surgical emergency with a narrow (<2 h) salvage window; delay results in irreversible flap loss and re-operative failure.

clinicalStem: A 4-month-old male infant is brought by his parents with a rapidly enlarging bright-red, raised, compressible lesion on his right upper eyelid that has grown from a small red spot at birth over six weeks. The lesion now partially occludes the visual axis. His parents report it blanches with pressure and was not present at birth. Ophthalmology assessment confirms a 2 cm lesion obstructing approximately 40% of the pupil. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Oral propranolol therapy
Pulsed dye laser treatment
Intralesional triamcinolone injection
Surgical excision under general anaesthesia
Reassurance and watchful waiting as the lesion will involute by age 5
explanation: This is a proliferating-phase infantile haemangioma with visual axis obstruction, a high-risk indication requiring active treatment to prevent amblyopia. Oral propranolol (a non-selective beta-blocker) is the current first-line treatment for problematic infantile haemangiomas, having superseded systemic steroids due to superior efficacy, faster response, and a more favourable safety profile. Treatment arrests proliferation and accelerates involution.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: oral propranolol is the evidence-based first-line agent for high-risk infantile haemangiomas, including those causing visual axis obstruction.
Pulsed dye laser is useful for superficial residual telangiectasias or flat lesions after involution, not for a rapidly proliferating raised lesion threatening vision.
Intralesional steroids were previously used for localised lesions but are now second-line; they are inferior to propranolol and carry risks including fat atrophy and skin depigmentation.
Surgical excision under GA in a 4-month-old carries significant risks and is not first-line; surgery is reserved for complicated residual lesions or failures of medical therapy.
Watchful waiting is appropriate for uncomplicated haemangiomas away from critical structures, but vision-threatening lesions mandate active treatment to prevent irreversible amblyopia.
clinicalPearl: Infantile haemangioma obstructing the visual axis = treat urgently with oral propranolol to prevent amblyopia; watchful waiting is contraindicated.
keyClue: Rapidly growing compressible lesion partially occluding the visual axis in a 4-month-old — classic proliferating-phase infantile haemangioma
commonExamTrap: Choosing watchful waiting because haemangiomas involute — this is only appropriate for uncomplicated lesions; visual axis obstruction at this age causes deprivation amblyopia.
missedConcept: High-risk locations for infantile haemangioma (periocular, airway, large segmental) mandate active management, not observation.

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old girl is referred with a flat, unilateral, pink-to-red macular stain over the left trigeminal V1 distribution that has been present since birth. It does not blanch fully with pressure and has gradually darkened. Her parents report occasional brief seizures over the past three months. MRI brain with contrast is subsequently performed and shows leptomeningeal enhancement ipsilateral to the facial lesion. What is the most important immediate management priority?
options: Neurology referral and antiepileptic drug initiation for Sturge-Weber syndrome
Urgent pulsed dye laser treatment of the port-wine stain to prevent darkening
Ophthalmology referral for glaucoma screening as the only required intervention
Low-dose aspirin only and annual MRI surveillance
Reassure parents that seizures in this age group are benign febrile convulsions
explanation: This presentation — port-wine stain in the V1 trigeminal distribution, new seizures, and ipsilateral leptomeningeal enhancement on MRI — is diagnostic of Sturge-Weber syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis). The combination of active seizures and CNS vascular malformation requires urgent neurology referral and initiation of antiepileptic therapy to prevent progressive neurological injury and status epilepticus. This is the highest-priority intervention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Sturge-Weber syndrome with active seizures and leptomeningeal angioma requires urgent neurology input and antiepileptic therapy — neurological protection is the immediate priority.
Pulsed dye laser is the treatment for the cosmetic port-wine stain but is elective and not the immediate priority when the patient is having seizures from CNS involvement.
Ophthalmology referral for glaucoma is important in Sturge-Weber syndrome (25–50% develop glaucoma) but is not the immediate priority when the child is actively seizing.
Aspirin may be used adjunctively in Sturge-Weber to reduce thrombotic events but is not a substitute for antiepileptic therapy in a child with active seizures.
Dismissing the seizures as febrile convulsions when an MRI shows leptomeningeal enhancement is dangerous and ignores clear evidence of CNS vascular malformation.
clinicalPearl: Port-wine stain in V1 distribution + seizures + leptomeningeal enhancement = Sturge-Weber syndrome — neurology and antiepileptics are the immediate priority.
keyClue: V1 port-wine stain present since birth + new seizures + ipsilateral leptomeningeal enhancement on MRI
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on the skin lesion (laser) and missing the urgent neurological emergency of active seizures in Sturge-Weber syndrome.
missedConcept: Sturge-Weber syndrome involves three systems: skin (port-wine stain), CNS (leptomeningeal angioma/seizures), and eye (glaucoma) — neurological management always takes priority when seizures are present.
redFlagReason: Active seizures in the context of leptomeningeal angiomatosis (Sturge-Weber) carry risk of status epilepticus and progressive neurological deterioration; immediate antiepileptic treatment is required.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a pulsatile, warm, bluish-red cutaneous mass over the right cheek present since childhood that has recently started bleeding intermittently. Doppler ultrasound shows high-flow lesion with arteriovenous shunting. MRI confirms an arteriovenous malformation (AVM) classified as Schobinger stage II. She is haemodynamically stable. Which is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: Endovascular embolisation followed by surgical resection
Immediate surgical excision without prior embolisation
Systemic sirolimus therapy as definitive treatment
Observation only with repeat MRI in 12 months
Compression therapy and elevation to reduce arteriovenous flow
explanation: Cutaneous AVMs at Schobinger stage II (expansion — pulsation, thrill, warmth, and tortuous veins) with recurrent bleeding require active intervention. The standard of care for high-flow AVMs is pre-operative endovascular embolisation (to reduce intraoperative haemorrhage) followed within 24–48 hours by surgical resection. Embolisation alone is not curative as collateral flow re-establishes the AVM, and surgery without embolisation carries unacceptable haemorrhage risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: embolisation prior to resection is the standard of care for symptomatic facial AVMs — it reduces intraoperative blood loss by up to 90% and enables complete excision.
Immediate surgical excision of a high-flow AVM without prior embolisation risks catastrophic intraoperative haemorrhage from the enlarged feeding vessels.
Sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) is emerging for some vascular malformations (particularly complex low-flow or mixed) but is not established as definitive treatment for typical cutaneous AVMs.
Observation is appropriate for asymptomatic Schobinger stage I AVMs, but this patient has stage II with active bleeding — a clear indication for intervention.
Compression and elevation are adjuncts for low-flow malformations (venous/lymphatic); they have no role in reducing arteriovenous shunting in a high-flow AVM.
clinicalPearl: Symptomatic AVM (Schobinger stage II+): pre-operative embolisation then surgical excision within 24–48 hours — never embolise without planning resection as recanalisation is inevitable.
keyClue: Pulsatile, warm, high-flow lesion on Doppler showing AV shunting with active bleeding — Schobinger stage II AVM
commonExamTrap: Choosing embolisation alone as definitive treatment — AVMs recanalise after embolisation alone; resection must follow.
missedConcept: AVMs are high-flow lesions that recanalise after embolisation alone; the embolisation-then-resection sequence is the only curative approach.

topic: Burns — initial assessment and fluid resuscitation
subtopic: Parkland formula and burn percentage calculation
clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man (weight 70 kg) is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after sustaining burns from a house fire. He has full-thickness burns to the entire anterior trunk, both anterior thighs, and partial-thickness burns to the right forearm. Using the Rule of Nines, his total body surface area (TBSA) burned is calculated at 36%. He is alert but anxious, with BP 100/70 mmHg and HR 118 bpm. What volume of Hartmann's solution should be given in the first 8 hours using the Parkland formula?
explanation: The Parkland formula: 4 mL × weight (kg) × %TBSA burned = total fluid in first 24 hours. Here: 4 × 70 × 36 = 10,080 mL total over 24 hours. Half of this (5,040 mL) is given in the first 8 hours from the time of injury (not from arrival), and the remaining half over the following 16 hours. Given the patient arrived 30 minutes post-burn, the first 8-hour volume (5,040 mL) must be delivered by 7.5 hours from now.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: 4 × 70 × 36 = 10,080 mL total; half = 5,040 mL given in the first 8 hours from time of burn.
10,080 mL is the total 24-hour volume — this would be a dangerous overdose if given in the first 8 hours.
2,520 mL is one-quarter of the total (the 16-hour half divided by 2), not the correct 8-hour fraction.
3,600 mL corresponds to a smaller TBSA calculation error; the correct calculation gives 5,040 mL for the first 8 hours.
7,200 mL does not correspond to any correct application of the Parkland formula with these values.
clinicalPearl: Parkland formula: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA — half in first 8 hours (from time of burn, not arrival), half over next 16 hours using Hartmann's solution.
keyClue: 70 kg patient, 36% TBSA burn — applying Parkland: 4 × 70 × 36 = 10,080 mL total; 5,040 mL in first 8 hours
commonExamTrap: Giving the total 24-hour volume (10,080 mL) in the first 8 hours — only half is given in the first 8 hours; also timing starts from burn, not hospital arrival.
missedConcept: The 8-hour clock for the first half of Parkland fluid runs from the time of injury, not from ED arrival — fluid delivery must be front-loaded accordingly.
redFlagReason: Major burn with haemodynamic compromise (hypotension, tachycardia) requires immediate and correctly calculated fluid resuscitation; errors in Parkland formula timing cause burn shock.

topic: Burns — depth assessment and wound management
clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents after scalding her right hand with boiling water. Examination reveals a leathery, white, painless wound that does not blanch with pressure, with no blisters. The surrounding normal skin is painful to touch. There is no capillary refill in the affected area. What is the burn depth and what does this imply for management?
options: Full-thickness burn — will not heal by secondary intention and requires skin grafting
Superficial partial-thickness burn — will heal within 14 days with conservative dressings
Deep partial-thickness burn — may heal in 21 days without surgery if infection is prevented
Superficial epidermal burn — heals in 7 days with simple moisturisers
Full-thickness burn — can be allowed to heal by secondary intention over 4–6 weeks
explanation: Leathery, white, insensate, non-blanching wound with absent capillary refill and no blistering are the hallmarks of full-thickness (third-degree) burn, which destroys both the epidermis and the entire dermis including appendages. Because all epithelial elements are destroyed, spontaneous re-epithelialisation cannot occur and surgical debridement followed by skin grafting is required. Conservative healing of a full-thickness burn produces only contracture and severe scarring.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: leathery, white, insensate, non-blanching wound = full-thickness burn requiring surgical skin grafting — cannot re-epithelialise spontaneously.
Superficial partial-thickness burns are red, moist, blistering, and very painful (intact nerve endings); they heal within 14 days without surgery.

Deep partial-thickness burns are pale, reduced sensation, may have intact deep pain; they can sometimes heal by 21 days but often require surgery — this presentation is more severe (insensate, leathery).
Superficial epidermal (first-degree) burns are dry, red, and painful with no blistering or deep tissue involvement; they heal in 7 days without any surgery.
Allowing a full-thickness burn to heal by secondary intention results in severe contracture, prolonged healing, and poor functional and cosmetic outcome — grafting is mandated.
clinicalPearl: Full-thickness burn: leathery/white, insensate, non-blanching, no blisters — requires debridement and skin grafting; spontaneous healing is not possible.
keyClue: Leathery, white, painless wound with no capillary refill and no blanching — pathognomonic of full-thickness burn
commonExamTrap: Confusing the absence of pain with reassurance — full-thickness burns are painless because nerve endings are destroyed, not because the injury is mild.
missedConcept: Full-thickness burns destroy all dermal appendages, so no epithelial stem cells remain for spontaneous healing — skin grafting is the only way to achieve closure.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old man is brought from a house fire with burns to the face, singed nasal hairs, hoarse voice, and stridor on inspiration. His SpO2 is 94% on 15 L/min non-rebreather mask. Oral examination shows carbonaceous soot in the pharynx and erythema of the posterior pharynx. He is anxious and drooling. What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Immediate early endotracheal intubation before airway oedema causes complete obstruction
High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask and transfer to burns unit for assessment
Nebulised adrenaline to reduce laryngeal oedema and reassess in 30 minutes
Cricothyroidotomy as the first-line airway intervention for inhalation injury
IV dexamethasone to reduce airway oedema and facilitate spontaneous breathing
explanation: This patient has clinical signs of imminent airway obstruction from inhalation injury: stridor, hoarse voice, drooling, carbonaceous soot in the pharynx, singed nasal hairs, and facial burns with progressive oedema. Airway oedema from thermal and chemical injury progresses rapidly and can cause complete obstruction within minutes to hours. Early prophylactic intubation while the airway is still patent is the life-saving intervention; as oedema worsens, intubation becomes increasingly difficult and may become impossible, necessitating a surgical airway under far more dangerous circumstances.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: signs of imminent airway compromise (stridor, hoarse voice, drooling, soot, facial burns) mandate immediate endotracheal intubation before the airway closes completely.
High-flow oxygen alone and transfer are dangerously inadequate for a patient already in stridor with a progressive airway; intubation cannot be deferred.
Nebulised adrenaline provides only temporary relief and 30 minutes of observation is unacceptably dangerous when the patient already has stridor and drooling.
Cricothyroidotomy is a rescue technique when intubation has failed or is impossible — it is not the first-line intervention when endotracheal intubation is still feasible.
IV dexamethasone has no proven benefit in inhalation airway oedema (which is thermochemical, not allergic/inflammatory); it does not act quickly enough and delays definitive airway management.
clinicalPearl: Stridor + hoarse voice + facial burns + carbonaceous soot = imminent airway obstruction — intubate immediately; oedema progresses rapidly and the window closes.
keyClue: Stridor, hoarse voice, drooling, carbonaceous soot, and singed nasal hairs following house fire — cardinal signs of inhalation injury with impending airway obstruction
commonExamTrap: Choosing high-flow oxygen and observation — this delays intubation until the airway is too oedematous to intubate, converting a controlled intubation into a surgical emergency.
missedConcept: In inhalation injury, oedema progresses for hours after the burn — intubate early and electively, not when the patient is in extremis.
redFlagReason: Inhalation injury with stridor and drooling represents imminent airway obstruction — delay in intubation risks complete airway closure and asphyxiation.

subtopic: Circumferential limb burns and compartment syndrome
clinicalStem: A 50-year-old man sustained circumferential full-thickness burns to his right lower leg in an industrial explosion 6 hours ago. He has received appropriate Parkland resuscitation. On review the leg is increasingly tense, the foot is cold with absent pulses on Doppler, and he complains of excruciating pain on passive dorsiflexion of the toes. Sensation in the web space between the first and second toes is diminished. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Bedside escharotomy of the right lower leg
Four-compartment fasciotomy under general anaesthesia in theatre
Urgent CT angiogram of the right leg to assess vascular patency
Elevate the limb and increase IV fluid rate, then reassess in 1 hour
Apply a bivalved cast and reduce fluid rate to prevent further oedema
explanation: Circumferential full-thickness burns create a non-compliant eschar that cannot expand as deep tissue oedema accumulates during resuscitation, causing compartment syndrome. The clinical findings — absent Doppler pulses, cold foot, pain on passive stretch, diminished sensation in the first web space — confirm vascular and neural compromise. Escharotomy (longitudinal incisions through the eschar) must be performed immediately at the bedside to release the constricting eschar and restore perfusion. Fasciotomy is reserved if escharotomy alone is insufficient or if compartment syndrome exists independent of eschar.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: circumferential burn with vascular and neural compromise requires immediate bedside escharotomy — this is a limb-threatening emergency that cannot await theatre scheduling.

Fasciotomy is the next step if escharotomy alone fails to restore perfusion; it should not be the first intervention for a pure eschar-compression problem, but escharotomy can be done immediately at the bedside.
CT angiogram delays the urgent intervention; absent Doppler pulses and ischaemic signs in the context of circumferential burn make the diagnosis clinically evident without imaging.
Elevation (inappropriate for ischaemic limb — it reduces perfusion pressure) and increasing fluids will worsen oedema and delay the life-saving escharotomy.
Bivalved cast and reducing fluids is entirely inappropriate — the burn eschar, not the fluid rate, is causing external compression.
clinicalPearl: Circumferential full-thickness burn + absent pulses + pain on passive stretch = eschar-induced compartment syndrome — perform bedside escharotomy immediately.
keyClue: Absent Doppler pulses, cold foot, pain on passive stretch, reduced sensation following circumferential full-thickness burn
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT angiogram or waiting for theatre for fasciotomy when bedside escharotomy is the immediate, life-limb-saving intervention that must be done without delay.
missedConcept: Escharotomy releases the non-compliant eschar to decompress compartment pressure — it is a bedside emergency procedure, distinct from deep fasciotomy.
redFlagReason: Circumferential burn with absent distal pulses, ischaemia and neurological compromise represents acute compartment syndrome — failure to perform immediate escharotomy results in irreversible limb loss.

subtopic: Treatment selection based on lesion characteristics
clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man with a history of significant sun exposure presents with a 1.8 cm pearly, rolled-edge nodule with central ulceration on the right nasal ala, present for 18 months. Dermoscopy shows arborising telangiectasias. Shave biopsy confirms nodular basal cell carcinoma. He has no lymphadenopathy and imaging shows no metastatic disease. Which management provides the lowest recurrence rate for this high-risk location?
options: Mohs micrographic surgery
Wide local excision with 4 mm margins
Radiotherapy
Imiquimod 5% cream for 6 weeks
Curettage and electrodesiccation
explanation: Basal cell carcinoma on the nose — particularly the nasal ala and alar-facial sulcus — is classified as high-risk (H-zone face) because of its potential for deep local invasion along periosteum and cartilage, high recurrence rate with standard excision, and the need for tissue conservation. Mohs micrographic surgery provides complete peripheral and deep margin control with the lowest reported 5-year recurrence rates (approximately 1% for primary BCC) while maximising tissue preservation in this functionally and cosmetically critical location.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Mohs surgery is the gold standard for high-risk facial BCC (H-zone), offering the lowest recurrence rate with tissue-sparing technique.

Wide local excision with 4 mm margins achieves good results for low-risk BCC but has higher recurrence rates than Mohs on the H-zone face where margins are difficult to assess histologically in real time.
Radiotherapy is a valid alternative for patients who cannot tolerate surgery, but its 5-year recurrence rate is higher than Mohs, and the nose tolerates post-irradiation changes poorly.
Imiquimod is only indicated for small, superficial BCC on the trunk or limbs; it is not appropriate for a nodular BCC on the nasal ala (a high-risk site).

Curettage and electrodesiccation is appropriate only for small, low-risk, non-facial superficial BCCs; it is absolutely contraindicated on H-zone facial sites due to unacceptably high recurrence rates.
clinicalPearl: H-zone face BCC (nose, periocular, ear, temple, lip) = Mohs micrographic surgery for tissue preservation and the lowest recurrence rate.
keyClue: Nodular BCC on the nasal ala (H-zone) — high-risk location requiring margin-controlled surgery
commonExamTrap: Choosing wide excision with 4 mm margins as adequate — this is appropriate for low-risk BCC elsewhere on the face but not for H-zone where Mohs gives superior margin control and recurrence rates.
missedConcept: The H-zone of the face (nose, periocular, ear, lips) constitutes high-risk BCC territory where Mohs surgery is the treatment of choice due to tissue sparing and real-time margin assessment.

topic: Squamous cell carcinoma — high-risk features and management
clinicalStem: A 68-year-old immunosuppressed renal transplant recipient presents with a 2.5 cm indurated, firm nodule on the right temporal scalp. Excision biopsy reveals a poorly differentiated cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma with perineural invasion and a positive deep margin. MRI head shows perineural extension along the auriculotemporal nerve. He has no palpable lymphadenopathy and PET-CT shows no distant metastases. Following re-excision achieving clear margins, what adjuvant treatment is most appropriate?
options: Adjuvant radiotherapy to the primary site and regional nodal basin
Adjuvant chemotherapy with cisplatin alone
Surveillance only with 3-monthly clinical examination
Elective parotidectomy with selective neck dissection without radiotherapy
Immunotherapy with cemiplimab as first-line adjuvant treatment
explanation: This patient has a cutaneous SCC with multiple high-risk features conferring very high recurrence and metastatic risk: immunosuppression, poor differentiation, perineural invasion (a particularly adverse prognostic feature), and MRI-confirmed perineural extension along a named nerve. Following clear-margin re-excision, adjuvant radiotherapy to the primary site and draining nodal basin is the standard of care to address subclinical perineural spread along the nerve trunk and reduce regional nodal relapse in this high-risk scenario.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: adjuvant radiotherapy to the primary site and regional nodal basin is standard of care for high-risk cutaneous SCC with perineural invasion extending along named nerves.

Cisplatin-based chemotherapy may be used in unresectable or metastatic disease but is not standard adjuvant monotherapy for resected high-risk cutaneous SCC; radiotherapy is the backbone of adjuvant treatment.
Surveillance alone after clear-margin excision of an SCC with perineural spread along a named nerve is inadequate — the high-risk features mandate adjuvant treatment.

Elective parotidectomy and neck dissection may be considered for nodal staging or control, but without radiotherapy, the risk of local and regional recurrence from perineural disease remains unacceptably high.

Cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) is approved for advanced/unresectable cutaneous SCC but is not established as first-line adjuvant therapy after complete surgical excision in guidelines; radiotherapy remains standard.
clinicalPearl: Cutaneous SCC with perineural invasion along a named nerve = high-risk for recurrence — adjuvant radiotherapy to primary site and nodes after complete excision reduces recurrence.
keyClue: Poorly differentiated SCC with confirmed perineural extension along auriculotemporal nerve on MRI in an immunosuppressed patient
commonExamTrap: Choosing surveillance only after clear-margin re-excision — perineural spread along named nerves represents a high-risk feature that mandates adjuvant radiotherapy.
missedConcept: Perineural invasion in cutaneous SCC — especially extension along named nerves on imaging — is a high-risk feature requiring adjuvant radiotherapy regardless of clear surgical margins.
